section.title: Words, Friends, and Folly
section.sectionRationale: Another chapter of independent sentence proverbs from the first Solomonic collection, 10:1 to 22:16. Chapter 18 gathers sayings about isolation and community, the power of the tongue for ruin or refuge, and the difference between many shallow companions and the one friend who sticks closer than a brother.
section.thesis: Life and death are in the power of the tongue, and the isolated, opinionated fool ruins himself, while the humble who seek understanding, trust the LORD as a strong tower, and hold fast to a true friend find safety and life.
section.frameNote: Five clusters: the isolated fool and the fool's mouth; the tongue's power and the strong tower of the LORD's name; hearing before answering; the gift, the wealth, and the wife the LORD gives; the tongue that gives life or death, and the friend who sticks closer than a brother.

verses.text: He who isolates himself pursues selfish desires; he rebels against all sound judgment.
A fool does not delight in understanding, but only in airing his opinions.
With a wicked man comes contempt as well, and shame is accompanied by disgrace.
The words of a man's mouth are deep waters; the fountain of wisdom is a bubbling brook.

units.label: The tongue's power and the LORD's name
units.note: life and death in the mouth

verses.text: Showing partiality to the wicked is not good, nor is depriving the innocent of justice.
A fool's lips bring him strife, and his mouth invites a beating.
A fool's mouth is his ruin, and his lips are a snare to his soul.
The words of a gossip are like choice morsels that go down into the inmost being.
Whoever is slothful in his work is brother to him who destroys.
The name of the LORD is a {{migdal|strong tower}}; the righteous run to it and are safe.
A rich man's wealth is his fortified city; it is like a high wall in his imagination.
Before his downfall a man's heart is proud, but humility comes before honor.
He who answers a matter before he hears it, this is folly and disgrace to him.
The spirit of a man can endure his sickness, but who can survive a broken spirit?
The heart of the discerning acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks it out.

units.label: Gifts, quarrels, and the wife the LORD gives
units.note: the good things a man finds

verses.text: A man's gift opens doors for him, and brings him before great men.
The first to state his case seems right until another comes and cross-examines him.
Casting the lot ends quarrels and separates strong opponents.
An offended brother is harder to win than a fortified city, and disputes are like the bars of a castle.
From the fruit of his mouth a man's belly is filled; with the harvest from his lips he is satisfied.
Life and death are in the power of the {{lashon|tongue}}, and those who love it will eat its fruit.
He who finds a wife finds a good thing and obtains favor from the LORD.

units.label: A friend closer than a brother
units.note: the poor plead, the rich answer harshly

verses.text: The poor man pleads for mercy, but the rich man answers harshly.
A man of many companions may come to ruin, but there is a {{ohev|friend}} who stays closer than a brother.

migdal.gloss: Strong tower, a fortified place of refuge. Verse 10 names the LORD's own name as this tower, meaning His revealed character and presence, not a magic word, is where the righteous run and find real safety.

lashon.gloss: Tongue, the organ of speech and, by extension, speech itself. Verse 21 says life and death are in its power, a sober claim that words build up or tear down with almost physical force, and that the speaker will eat the fruit of what the tongue produces.

ohev.gloss: One who loves, a loving friend. Verse 24 sets this steady, loving friend against a crowd of many companions who may not endure, describing a bond closer than blood family, one Scripture ultimately locates in Christ.

basic.context: Chapter 18 gathers proverbs about isolation, speech, and friendship. It opens by warning against the man who cuts himself off to chase his own opinions, moves through the terrifying power of the tongue for both ruin and refuge, and ends with the tender claim that some friends love more faithfully than family.
basic.hermeneutics: These are wisdom generalizations, true patterns of how life usually works, not iron laws. Not every isolated person is a fool and not every rich man is safe, but the proverbs press readers to notice how pride, isolation, and a careless tongue tend toward ruin, while humility, community, and careful speech tend toward safety.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD's name is a migdal, a strong tower, verse 10, where the righteous find real refuge, not a magic charm. The lashon, the tongue, verse 21, holds life and death, a vivid Hebrew way of saying speech has almost physical consequence. And the ohev, the one who loves, verse 24, describes a friend more constant than a blood brother.
basic.typology: The strong tower of the LORD's name, verse 10, anticipates Christ, in whose name alone there is salvation, Acts 4:12. The friend who sticks closer than a brother, verse 24, foreshadows Jesus, who calls His people friends and never abandons them, even to death.
basic.christ: Christ is the name that is now the strong tower into which the righteous run, since salvation is found in no other name, Acts 4:12, and He is the friend who sticks closer than a brother, laying down His life for those He loves, John 15:13. Where chapter 18 warns that the tongue holds life and death, verse 21, the Word made flesh speaks only life, and by His words the dead are raised.
basic.god: God's own name is the place of safety, verse 10, a claim about His character, not a formula; He is trustworthy, and those who run to Him in truth are kept safe. He hates partiality in judgment, verse 5, showing that His justice reaches into the smallest human courtroom disputes as much as the grandest questions of eternity.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 4:12 declares salvation in no other name, echoing the strong tower of 18:10. James 3:1 to 12 develops the tongue's power for blessing and cursing. John 15:13 to 15 names Jesus as the friend who lays down His life. Proverbs 27:6 pairs with 18:24 to distinguish faithful wounds from false flattery.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 18 warns against isolation, pride, and a reckless tongue, and commends humility, careful listening, and a friend who loves at all times. The whole Bible answers with Jesus, the name that is our strong tower and the friend who sticks closer than a brother, whose words alone hold the power of life.

context.literary: Chapter 18 continues the first Solomonic collection of sentence proverbs, 10:1 to 22:16, loosely grouped around isolation versus community, the tongue's power, and true versus false security, ending on the memorable image of the faithful friend.
context.historical: In a tightly knit village and clan culture, isolating oneself from community counsel, verse 1, and casting lots to settle disputes, verse 18, reflect real social practices; wealth's false sense of security, verse 11, spoke directly to a world where fortified cities were the visible symbol of safety.
context.canonical: The strong tower of the LORD's name, verse 10, threads through the Psalms, for example Psalm 61:3, and culminates in the New Testament's insistence that salvation is found in the name of Jesus alone, Acts 4:12.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn against self-isolating pride and careless speech, and to commend humble listening, careful words, trust in the LORD as refuge, and faithful friendship as the path of wisdom and safety.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses like 13 and 15 prescribe behavior directly, listen before you answer, seek out knowledge, while verses like 11 describe a common but false human perception, the rich man imagines his wealth is a high wall. Readers must distinguish direct commands from honest descriptions of mistaken human confidence.

debates.question: Is verse 22, 'he who finds a wife finds a good thing,' a promise that marriage guarantees blessing?

views.label: A near-universal promise of blessing in marriage
views.case: The verse's positive, unqualified tone can be read as commending marriage itself as an unmixed good and a mark of divine favor.

views.label: A general wisdom observation, not an unconditional guarantee
views.case: Like other Proverbs sayings, it observes a common pattern, a good marriage is a genuine gift from the LORD, without promising that every marriage or every finding of a spouse is trouble free; Proverbs elsewhere, 21:9 and 19, acknowledges painful marriages exist.

debates.bearing: Read as general wisdom rather than unconditional guarantee, the proverb still honors marriage as a genuine good from the LORD while leaving room for the real hardships Proverbs elsewhere names.

study.originalLanguages: Migdal, strong tower, verse 10, pictures the LORD's revealed name, His character in covenant, as a literal place of refuge for the righteous. Lashon, tongue, verse 21, carries the weight of life and death in Hebrew idiom, meaning speech has almost bodily consequence for good or ill. And ohev, one who loves, verse 24, names a friend whose loyalty outlasts even family bonds.

typology.type: The strong tower of the LORD's name, 18:10
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the name given for salvation, in whom alone refuge is found, Acts 4:12.

typology.type: The friend who sticks closer than a brother, 18:24
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who calls His disciples friends and lays down His life for them, John 15:13-15.

study.christ: The strong tower of 18:10 finds its fulfillment in Jesus, since there is salvation in no other name under heaven, Acts 4:12; to run into that tower is to trust in Him. And the friend who sticks closer than a brother, 18:24, is supremely Christ, who did not merely stay near His people in hardship but died for them, John 15:13, turning a wisdom proverb about loyal friendship into a portrait of the gospel itself. Even the chapter's warning that the tongue holds life and death, verse 21, points to the One whose word raises the dead and whose gospel is the power of life to all who believe.
study.god: God's own name, not a formula but His revealed character, is the place the righteous run to and are kept safe, verse 10. He detests partiality in judgment, verse 5, holding every human court accountable to His own perfect justice, and He gives good things, including a good wife, as gifts of favor, verse 22, reminding His people that ordinary blessings are traced back to His hand.

crossReferences.note: Salvation is found in no other name, the ultimate strong tower of Proverbs 18:10.
Develops the tongue's power to bless or curse, echoing life and death in 18:21.
Jesus calls His disciples friends and lays down His life, fulfilling the friend of 18:24.
Faithful are the wounds of a friend, complementing the loyal friend of 18:24.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 18 warns against pride, isolation, and a careless tongue, and commends humility, careful listening, refuge in the LORD, and steadfast friendship. Every one of these threads is gathered up in Jesus, the name that is our strong tower and the friend who sticks closer than a brother, whose very words hold the power of life.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, what happens to the man who isolates himself?

options.text: He pursues selfish desires and rebels against all sound judgment.
options.note: Isolation from community counsel is tied to selfish rebellion against wisdom.

options.text: He becomes wiser through solitude.
options.note: The verse presents isolation negatively, not as a path to wisdom.

options.text: He avoids all conflict successfully.
options.note: The verse's concern is selfish rebellion, not conflict avoidance.

options.text: He gains sound judgment through independence.
options.note: This reverses the verse; isolation is linked to rebelling against sound judgment, not gaining it.

quiz.q: What does verse 10 call the name of the LORD?

options.text: A strong tower where the righteous run and are safe.
options.note: The LORD's revealed name and character are pictured as a fortified refuge.

options.text: A fortified city belonging to the rich.
options.note: That image describes the rich man's wealth in verse 11, a false security by contrast.

options.text: A weapon against enemies.
options.note: The image is refuge, not a weapon.

options.text: A high wall in a man's imagination.
options.note: This phrase describes the rich man's false confidence in verse 11, not the true safety of verse 10.

quiz.q: What does verse 21 say is in the power of the tongue?

options.text: Life and death.
options.note: Speech has weighty, almost physical consequence for good or ill.

options.text: Only death, never life.
options.note: The verse names both life and death as possible fruits of speech.

options.text: Wealth and poverty only.
options.note: The verse's terms are life and death, broader than material wealth.

options.text: Mostly life, with death being rare.
options.note: The verse presents both possibilities without weighting one as rare.

options.text: Answering a matter before hearing it out, calling this folly and disgrace.
options.note: The proverb condemns hasty, premature answers.

options.text: Listening too long before responding.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's actual warning.

options.text: Speaking with eloquence.
options.note: The verse's concern is timing and listening, not eloquence.

options.text: Answering quickly but politely.
options.note: Politeness does not address the core problem the verse names, answering before hearing.

quiz.q: How does verse 24 describe a certain kind of friend?

options.text: A friend who stays closer than a brother, unlike many companions who may lead to ruin.
options.note: The verse contrasts shallow, numerous companions with one deeply loyal friend.

options.text: A friend who is useful only when convenient.
options.note: This is the opposite of the loyal, constant friend the verse describes.

options.text: Many companions are always better than one close friend.
options.note: The verse actually warns that many companions may lead to ruin.

options.text: A friend who is somewhat close, similar to an acquaintance.
options.note: The verse's language is much stronger, closer than a brother, not merely acquaintance level.

questions: Verse 1 warns against isolating yourself to chase your own opinions. Where might you be cutting yourself off from wise counsel right now?
The tongue holds life and death, verse 21. What is one recent conversation where your words gave life, and one where they may have brought harm?
The LORD's name is a strong tower, verse 10. What are you running to for safety instead of Him this season, wealth, reputation, control?
Verse 13 warns against answering before listening. Who in your life deserves a slower, more attentive hearing from you?
Verse 24 names a friend who stays closer than a brother. Are you cultivating that kind of costly, constant friendship, or settling for many shallow companions?

translationNotes.note: 'Strong tower' translates migdal oz, a fortified tower; the phrase pictures the LORD's revealed name, His character and covenant promises, as a literal refuge, not a magic incantation.
The Hebrew places 'death and life' before 'tongue,' emphasizing the weight of the claim; some translations reorder for English flow, as BSB does here with 'life and death.'

The Hebrew behind 'a man of many companions' can also suggest 'a man who has friends must show himself friendly,' a reading some older translations follow, though the sense of contrast with the one loyal friend remains the same.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 18. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
